fields.name: 13. Refutation
fields.content-01:
Introduction (5 minutes)

‍

  • Explain the concept of refutation and its importance in debates.
  • Provide examples of common weaknesses in arguments (e.g., lack of evidence, logical fallacies, or generalizations).
  • Briefly describe the activity and its rules.

‍

Setup and Instructions (5 minutes)

‍

  • Divide students into groups (or work as a whole class, depending on size).
  • Give every student their own argument (statement)
  • Explain the sequence: The first student will deliver their argument, evert next student will refute the previous argument, and then present their own.

‍

Activity: Refutation Chain (30 minutes)

‍

  • Step 1: The first student starts by presenting their argument (e.g., 1-2 minutes).
  • Step 2: The second student identifies a weakness in the first argument, explains it, and then presents their own argument (e.g., 2-3 minutes).
  • Step 3: The third student refutes the second argument, explains their reasoning, and presents a new argument.
  • Continue until all students have participated.
  • Note: Allow a brief pause (30 seconds) for students to gather their thoughts if needed.

‍

Debrief and Reflection (5 minutes)

‍

  • Discuss with students:
    • What did they find most challenging: refuting or presenting their own arguments?
    • Were there any arguments that were particularly strong or weak? Why?
  • Highlight the importance of balancing quick thinking with structured reasoning in debates.
fields.content-02:
Objectives

‍

  • To develop students' ability to refute arguments effectively and think critically under time constraints.
  • To enhance active listening skills by requiring students to respond directly to their peers' arguments.
  • To practice both argumentation (building an argument) and refutation (pointing out weaknesses in others' arguments).
fields.content-03:
Learning outcomes

‍

By the end of the workshop, students will be able to:

  • Identify weaknesses or logical flaws in others' arguments.
  • Respond to opposing arguments quickly and effectively.
  • Build their own arguments while anticipating potential counterarguments.
  • Work collaboratively and think critically in a structured setting.
fields.content-04:
Materials and tools

‍

  • A list of debate arguments, prepared in advance or brainstormed as a group (examples provided at the end of this lesson).
  • A timer or stopwatch to ensure timed responses.
fields.content-05:
Assessment

‍

  • Observations of student participation: Are they actively engaging in refutation and argumentation?
  • Quality of refutation: Are students identifying relevant weaknesses in arguments?
  • Reflection responses during the debrief.

‍

Tips and Tricks

‍

  • Use topics that are familiar and engaging to students to make the exercise more dynamic.
  • Encourage respectful and constructive refutation to maintain a supportive environment.
fields.extra-text:
ATTACHMENT: Examples of arguments that can be used

‍

  • "Climate change is the most critical global issue of our time."
  • "Fast food restaurants should be banned near schools."
  • "Social media platforms should regulate fake news more strictly."
  • "Part-time jobs during school help students become more responsible."
  • "Learning how to manage personal finances should be a mandatory subject in school."
  • "Mobile phones should be allowed in classrooms for educational purposes."
